questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
I. No virus can survive in ocean waters.
II. No virus can infect bacteria.
III. No virus can change the cellular transcriptional activity in host cells.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: I. No virus can survive in ocean waters. II. No virus can infect bacteria. III. No virus can change the cellular transcriptional activity in host cells. How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Statement I is incorrect:**Viruses are abundant in ocean waters. Oceans are estimated to contain billions of viruses, making them one of the most virus-rich environments on Earth. - The vast majority of these are bacteriophages, which infect marine bacteria. - These viruses play a key role in regulating microbial populations, nutrient cycling, and even in carbon cycling, by influencing the death and lysis (breaking down) of microbial cells. - Marine viruses are an active area of research in marine biology, virology, and climate science.**

Statement II is incorrect:**There is a well-known class of viruses called bacteriophages (or simply phages) that specifically infect bacteria. These are widely used in research and have even found therapeutic applications (called phage therapy) to combat antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections. Bacteriophages attach to bacterial cells, inject their genetic material, and hijack the bacterial machinery to replicate themselves.**

Statement III is incorrect:** Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites, meaning they rely on host cell machinery to replicate. To achieve this, many viruses manipulate and alter the host cell’s transcriptional activity (and other cellular processes) to favor viral gene expression and replication. This can involve activating or repressing host genes, hijacking host transcription factors, or introducing their own transcriptional machinery.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: This empty-looking space in plant cells helps them to store important substances, get rid of waste and maintain the shape of the cell. This gives strength and support to the plant. In animal cells, they are usually not present, if present, they are usually small.
The above paragraph best describes which of the following?
questions.questionText: This empty-looking space in plant cells helps them to store important substances, get rid of waste and maintain the shape of the cell. This gives strength and support to the plant. In animal cells, they are usually not present, if present, they are usually small. The above paragraph best describes which of the following?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.**

Option a is incorrect: **Centriole is**present in all the animal cells**(but not in Amoeba),**located just outside the nucleus**. It is cylindrical, 0.5 μm in length and without a membrane. It has its own DNA and RNA and therefore it is self duplicating. Centrioles are involved in cell division. They give orientation to the ‘mitotic spindle’ which forms during cell division.**

Option b is incorrect: Peroxisomes are found both in plant and animal cells. **They are found in the green leaves of higher plants. They participate in oxidation of substrates resulting in the formation of hydrogen peroxide. They often contain a central core of crystalline material called nucleoid composed of urate oxidase crystals. They are** involved in **photorespiration in plant cells. **They**bring about fat metabolism in cells. **

Option c is incorrect: Endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is a network of short tubular interconnected structures **scattered in the cytoplasm. ER divides the inner region of the cell into luminal (inside ER) and extra-luminal (cytoplasm) parts. ER is of two types: rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) and smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER). RER has a rough surface due to the presence of ribosomes. It is the**site of protein synthesis**. It also**transports proteins to the Golgi apparatus.**SER lacks ribosomes and is involved in** synthesis of lipids. **

Option d is correct:**Vacuoles are membrane-bound sacs which contain water or dilute salt solutions.**Plant cells have **a large, empty-looking space called a vacuole. **These vacuoles are filled with cell sap and help in maintaining the rigidity and turgidity of the cell by regulating the water balance. This helps the plant cell**store important substances, get rid **of waste, and maintain the shape of the cell. **This gives strength and support to the plant.**In animal cells, vacuoles are **usually not present, **if present, they are usually small. These small vacuoles store certain substances dissolved in water.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Consider the following statements with reference to yeasts:
I. They belong to a group of microorganisms called fungi.
II. Carbon dioxide is evolved from food materials spoiled by yeasts.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements with reference to yeasts: I. They belong to a group of microorganisms called fungi. II. Carbon dioxide is evolved from food materials spoiled by yeasts. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Yeasts are large, single-celled organisms that rarely form filaments. They are ovoid or spheroid and reproduce by budding or fission.**

Statement I is correct: **Yeast is a type of microorganism that**belongs to a group of microorganisms, called fungi. Yeast **is a unicellular fungus. **They are widely distributed in nature. They are somewhat larger than bacteria. The cell length is about 10mm and the diameter is about a third of this. Most yeast is spherical or ellipsoidal. Yeasts that multiply by means of 'budding' are known as 'true yeasts'.**

Statement II is correct: **Like other organisms,**yeast also respires**and breaks down food to release energy for their growth and carry out life processes. During this process,**carbon dioxide is released, **which forms bubbles that make the dough soft and fluffy.**Yeast also produces a small amount of alcohol **during this process, which gives the dough a slightly different smell. This special property of yeast is used in the process of making breads, cakes, etc.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells, consider the following statements:
I. Unlike prokaryotic cells, eukaryotic cells have a membrane-bound nucleus.
II. Unlike prokaryotic cells, cytoplasm is present in eukaryotic cells.
III. Prokaryotic cells are generally smaller and multiply more rapidly than the eukaryotic cells.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells, consider the following statements: I. Unlike prokaryotic cells, eukaryotic cells have a membrane-bound nucleus. II. Unlike prokaryotic cells, cytoplasm is present in eukaryotic cells. III. Prokaryotic cells are generally smaller and multiply more rapidly than the eukaryotic cells. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.**Cells are classified into two basic types-Eukaryotes (cells that possess a well-formed nucleus enclosed by a nuclear membrane) and Prokaryotes (cells that lack a well-defined, membrane-bound nucleus).**Statement I and III are correct: Cells that have membrane bound nuclei are called eukaryotic whereas cells that lack **a membrane bound nucleus are prokaryotic. **Eukaryotic cells have distinct nucleus with well formed nuclear membranes. In the case of prokaryotic cells, the nucleus is not distinct; it is in the form of a nuclear zone ‘nucleoid’ and the nuclear membrane is absent.**Prokaryotic cells are generally smaller and multiply more rapidly than the **eukaryotic cells. **

Statement II is incorrect:**In both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, a semi-fluid matrix called cytoplasm occupies **the volume of the cell. **The cytoplasm is the main arena of cellular activities in both the plant and animal cells. Various chemical reactions occur in it to keep the cell in the ‘living state’.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Consider the following statements in respect of probiotics:
1. Probiotics are made of both bacteria and yeast.
2. The organisms in probiotics are found in foods we ingest but they do not naturally occur in our gut.
3. Probiotics help in the digestion of milk sugars.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements in respect of probiotics: 1. Probiotics are made of both bacteria and yeast. 2. The organisms in probiotics are found in foods we ingest but they do not naturally occur in our gut. 3. Probiotics help in the digestion of milk sugars. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**Probiotics are live microorganisms that are intended to have health benefits when consumed or applied to the body. They can be found in yogurt and other fermented foods, dietary supplements, and beauty products.**

Statement 1 is correct: **Probiotics are a combination of**live beneficial bacteria and/or yeasts**.**

Statement 2 is incorrect: **Probiotics**live naturally in our body.**L. acidophilus is a probiotic bacterium that naturally **occurs in the human gut. **The most common place linked to beneficial microbes is our gut (mostly large intestines), but there are other places like mouth, urinary tract, skin, lungs etc where these probiotics are found.**

Statement 3 is correct: **Yogurt is a well-known food source of probiotics. Certain strains of bacteria in yogurt have ß-D-galactosidase, which is an enzyme that helps**break down lactose in dairy products into the sugar's glucose and **galactose**. Thus, Probiotics help in the digestion of milk sugars.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to diseases and their causative agents, consider the following pairs:
Disease Caused by
I. Measles Virus
II. Cholera Worms
III. Typhoid Bacteria
IV. Malaria Protozoa
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to diseases and their causative agents, consider the following pairs: Disease Caused by I. Measles Virus II. Cholera Worms III. Typhoid Bacteria IV. Malaria Protozoa How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Pair I is correct: Measles **Measles is caused by the Measles virus**, which belongs to the Paramyxoviridae family. - It is a highly contagious viral disease that spreads through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It affects the skin and respiratory tract. - Being viral in nature, it does not respond to antibiotics and is mainly prevented through**vaccination (MMR vaccine**).**Pair II is incorrect: Cholera **Cholera is not caused by worms. **It is an**acute diarrhoeal disease caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae (a Bacteria).**The infection spreads mainly through contaminated water or food and leads to severe watery diarrhoea and dehydration. -**Worms cause diseases **such as** ascariasis, filariasis (elephantiasis), and hookworm infection, not cholera.**Pair III is correct: Typhoid **Typhoid fever is**caused by bacterium Salmonella typhi.**- It spreads through contaminated food and water, especially in areas with poor sanitation and affects the intestinal tract and bloodstream. - Since it is a bacterial disease, it**can be treated with appropriate antibiotics**.**Pair IV is correct: Malaria **Malaria is caused by protozoan parasites **of the genus**Plasmodium**(such as**P. vivax, P. falciparum). **- These protozoa are transmitted to humans through the bite of an infected**female Anopheles mosquito. **- The protozoa multiply inside liver cells and red blood cells, leading to periodic fever and chills. - Being a protozoan disease, malaria requires**specific anti-malarial drugs**, not antibiotics.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: Antibiotics are ineffective in the treatment of Hepatitis A infection.
Statement II: Hepatitis A is a disease caused by a virus.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: Antibiotics are ineffective in the treatment of Hepatitis A infection. Statement II: Hepatitis A is a disease caused by a virus. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct:**Antibiotics**are medicines**specifically developed to destroy or inhibit the growth of bacteria**. They act by targeting bacterial structures and metabolic processes such as cell wall formation, protein synthesis, and DNA replication**. **Antibiotics kill the bacteria that might have caused the disease. Antibiotics work only against bacterial infections because they target parts of bacterial cells that are different from human or other animal cells. They do not work against viruses or diseases caused by protozoa.**Hepatitis A infection does not involve bacteria;**therefore,**antibiotics do not play any role in the treatment of Hepatitis A**.**

Statement II is correct: Hepatitis A is caused by the Hepatitis A virus **(HAV), a virus that infects liver cells and spreads mainly through the faeco-oral route, often via contaminated food or water.**Relationship between the statements: **The fact that**Hepatitis A is caused by a virus (Statement II) is the fundamental reason why antibiotics are ineffective **against it (Statement I).**Since antibiotics effectively act only on bacteria and not on viruses, the viral nature of Hepatitis A directly explains the lack of effectiveness of antibiotics in its treatment. Hence,** Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I. **

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: When iodine solution is added to a food substance and it turns blue-black in colour, this reaction primarily indicates the presence of which of the following in food substance?
questions.questionText: When iodine solution is added to a food substance and it turns blue-black in colour, this reaction primarily indicates the presence of which of the following in food substance?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **When iodine solution is added to a food substance containing starch, it produces a blue-black colour.**- This happens because iodine molecules get trapped inside the helical (spiral) structure of**amylose,**which is a major component of starch. - This interaction forms a coloured complex, resulting in the characteristic blue-black appearance. - This reaction is known as the**iodine test for starch**and is**commonly used to detect the presence of starch in food **materials. **

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: In eukaryotic cells, the organelle that serves as the principal site for energy release through aerobic cellular respiration is:
questions.questionText: In eukaryotic cells, the organelle that serves as the principal site for energy release through aerobic cellular respiration is:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Option d is correct: **In eukaryotic cells,**mitochondria**are known as the**powerhouses of the cell**because they are the**main sites of aerobic cellular respiration**. During this process, organic molecules such as**glucose are broken down**in the presence of oxygen to**release energy**which gets stored in the form of**ATP**(adenosine triphosphate) for ready use.**

Option a is incorrect: Golgi apparatus: **It is mainly involved in the**modification, sorting, and packaging of proteins and lipids**for secretion or transport within or outside the cell. It**does not participate in energy production. **

Option b is incorrect: Ribosomes: **Ribosomes are the**sites of protein synthesis**, where amino acids are assembled into polypeptide chains. They have**no **role in cellular respiration or ATP generation**.**

Option c is incorrect: Endoplasmic reticulum: **The**rough endoplasmic reticulum**is associated with**protein synthesis and transport**, while the**smooth endoplasmic **reticulum**is involved in**steroid synthesis **and storing carbohydrates,** not in aerobic energy production. **

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Consider the following cell organelles:
I. Cell wall
II. Plastids
III. Golgi apparatus
IV. Cytoplasm
How many of the above are generally present in plant cells but absent in animal cells?
questions.questionText: Consider the following cell organelles: I. Cell wall II. Plastids III. Golgi apparatus IV. Cytoplasm How many of the above are generally present in plant cells but absent in animal cells?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**.**Plant cells and animal cells**are the**two major types of eukaryotic cells**that form the structural and functional units of plants and animals respectively.**Both possess a well-defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles**such as mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, ribosomes and cytoplasm. However, they differ in structure and function due to the distinct lifestyles and nutritional requirements of plants and animals.** Option I is correct: Cell wall **The**cell wall**is a rigid, non-living outer layer**located outside the plasma membrane in plant cells**. It is primarily composed of**cellulose **and provides structural support, rigidity, shape, and protection to plant cells.**Animal cells do not have a cell wall**; instead, they are bounded only by a flexible cell membrane or plasma membrane. Hence, the**cell wall is**present in plant cells, but absent in animal cells.** Option II is correct:**Plastids **Plastids are**double-membrane-bound organelles found exclusively in plant cells and some algae**. They are responsible for key metabolic and biosynthetic functions and include chloroplasts (for photosynthesis), chromoplasts (for pigment storage), and leucoplasts (for storage of starch, proteins, and fats).**Animal cells lack plastids**because they do not perform photosynthesis. Therefore,**plastids**are**present only in plant cells and absent in animal cells**.** Option III is incorrect: Golgi apparatus **The Golgi apparatus is** present in both plant and animal cells. **It is involved in the modification, packaging, and transport of proteins and lipids. In plant cells, the Golgi bodies are often referred to as**dictyosomes**, but they are structurally and functionally the same organelle**. **Since it** occurs in both cell types, it is not exclusive to plant cells. ** Option IV is incorrect: Cytoplasm **The cytoplasm is the main arena of cellular activities in both the plant and animal cells. Various chemical reactions occur in it to keep the cell in the ‘living state’. Cytoplasm is the semi-fluid, jelly-like substance**present between the plasma **membrane and the nucleus. **It contains cell organelles and is the site of most metabolic and biochemical reactions. Since cytoplasm is essential for basic cellular functioning,**it is present in all living cells, including both plants and **animals.  Knowledge Base:**Differences between Plant Cell and Animal Cell**Plant Cell **Animal Cell **Cellulose cell walls are**present external to the cell membrane.**No cell wall**; outermost structure is the cell membrane (plasma membrane).**Vacuoles**are usually**large and centrally located.**Vacuoles are**generally absent**; if present, they are usually small.**Plastids are present. **Plastids are absent. **Golgi body is present**in the form of units known as dictyosomes.**Golgi body is well developed**and consists of flattened sacs called cisternae.**Centriole is absent. **Centriole is present. **

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Consider the following:
1. Bacteria
2. Fungi
3. Virus
Which of the above can be cultured in artificial/synthetic medium?
questions.questionText: Consider the following: 1. Bacteria 2. Fungi 3. Virus Which of the above can be cultured in artificial/synthetic medium?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**Bacteria and Fungi can be cultured in artificial/synthetic medium, but Viruses cannot.** Option 1 is correct: Bacteria: **Bacteria can be grown in artificial or synthetic laboratory media. These media provide the necessary nutrients, salts, and conditions for bacterial growth and can be tailored to specific bacterial species or strains.** Option 2 is correct: Fungi:**Fungi can also be cultured in artificial or synthetic media. These media typically contain sources of carbon, nitrogen, vitamins, and other essential nutrients required for fungal growth.** Option 3 is incorrect: Virus: **Viruses are not considered living organisms on their own. They are obligate intracellular parasites, meaning they cannot replicate outside a host cell. Therefore, they cannot be cultured in artificial media.
Instead, they require a host organism’s cells to replicate.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: The tetanus shot or tetanus vaccine, often administered after an injury to prevent infection, is produced from
which one of the following?
questions.questionText: The tetanus shot or tetanus vaccine, often administered after an injury to prevent infection, is produced from which one of the following?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Tetanus is caused by a toxin called tetanospasmin**, which**is produced by the bacterium Clostridium tetani.**This bacterium commonly enters the body through deep wounds, cuts, or injuries contaminated with soil, dust, or rusted objects. The infection leads to**painful muscle contractions, **particularly of the jaw and neck muscle, and is commonly known as “**lockjaw”**. - The**tetanus shot or vaccine**(Tetanus Toxoid) is**prepared from this toxin**after it has been inactivated (detoxified)**using chemicals such as formaldehyde. - The**inactivated toxin**, known as a**toxoid**, loses its disease-causing property but retains its antigenic nature. - When the toxoid is injected into the body, it**stimulates **the** immune system to produce specific antibodies against **the** tetanus toxin. **These antibodies remain in the blood and provide protection. - If the person later gets infected with Clostridium tetani, the already-formed antibodies immediately neutralize the toxin, thereby preventing symptoms of tetanus. Thus, the**tetanus shot contains an inactivated bacterial toxin** that safely induces long-term immunity.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to differences between xylem and phloem tissues in plants, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. Xylem primarily transports water and minerals from roots to other parts of the plant, whereas phloem primarily transports prepared food from leaves to different parts of the plant.
II. Transport through xylem is mainly bidirectional, whereas transport through phloem is always unidirectional.
III. Most of the structural elements of xylem are dead at maturity, while most structural elements of phloem are living tissues.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to differences between xylem and phloem tissues in plants, which of the following statements is/are correct? I. Xylem primarily transports water and minerals from roots to other parts of the plant, whereas phloem primarily transports prepared food from leaves to different parts of the plant. II. Transport through xylem is mainly bidirectional, whereas transport through phloem is always unidirectional. III. Most of the structural elements of xylem are dead at maturity, while most structural elements of phloem are living tissues. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Vascular tissues**are specialized conducting tissues in plants that help in the transport of water, minerals, and food, and also provide mechanical support. They form the plant’s internal transport system and are mainly of**two types: xylem **and phloem**. Together,**xylem and phloem form vascular bundles**, which are present in roots and stems. These tissues play a crucial role in the growth, survival, and proper functioning of higher plants.**

Statement I is correct:**Xylem and phloem**are the**two main vascular tissues in plants**with distinct transport functions. -**Xylem**is responsible for**transporting water and dissolved mineral salts from roots to**the stem, leaves, and other aerial**parts of the plant**. -**Phloem transports prepared food**(photosynthates) synthesised in the**leaves to different parts of the plant. **

Statement II is incorrect:**Transport through xylem is essentially unidirectional**, occurring mainly from roots to aerial parts due to root pressure, capillary action, and transpiration pull. In contrast,**phloem transport is bidirectional**, as food can move both upward and downward depending on source– sink relationships.**

Statement III is correct: **Xylem is a conducting tissue which conducts water and salts upward from roots to leaves. Xylem is composed of (a) Tracheids, (b) Vessels (c) Fibres and (d) Xylem Parenchyma.**Most structural elements of xylem**, such as tracheids, vessels, and xylem fibres (except Xylem Parenchyma)**are dead at maturity**, providing efficient conduction and mechanical support. Phloem is composed of (a) Sieve tube element (b) Companion cells (c) Phloem fibre and (d) Phloem Parenchyma. In contrast to xylem,**most phloem elements**, such as sieve tube elements and companion cells and Phloem Parenchyma (except phloem fibres)**are living tissues**, which are essential for active food transport.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: Bajra (Pearl Millet) and Ragi (Finger Millet) have a lower glycaemic index as compared to wheat and rice.
Statement II: Bajra (Pearl Millet) and Ragi (Finger Millet) are gluten-free cereals.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: Bajra (Pearl Millet) and Ragi (Finger Millet) have a lower glycaemic index as compared to wheat and rice. Statement II: Bajra (Pearl Millet) and Ragi (Finger Millet) are gluten-free cereals. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct:**Bajra (pearl millet)**and**ragi (finger millet)**have a**lower glycaemic index (GI) than wheat and **rice.**The glycaemic index indicates how quickly a food raises blood glucose levels after consumption. -**Bajra and ragi**are**whole millets**and**contain higher dietary fibre, complex carbohydrates**, and a more resistant starch structure, which**slow down**digestion and the**absorption of glucose into the bloodstream**. - As a result, they cause a**more gradual rise in blood sugar levels compared to rice and wheat**.**

Statement II is correct: Bajra and ragi are naturally gluten-free cereals, **meaning** they lack gluten-forming proteins **such as** gliadin and glutenin **that are found in wheat, barley and related grains. This characteristic makes them nutritionally important for individuals suffering from celiac disease or non-celiac gluten sensitivity, and it also broadens their role in diversified and inclusive diets. However,**Statement II does not explain Statement I. **The**absence of gluten does not determine the glycaemic index of a food**. - Glycaemic index depends primarily on factors such as fibre content, degree of processing, starch composition, and rate of digestion, rather than the presence or absence of gluten proteins. Therefore, while**both statements are individually correct**, the**reason given in Statement II does not account for the **lower glycaemic index mentioned in Statement I. **Hence, the**correct answer is b) Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain **Statement I. **

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Which one of the following is synthesised in human body that dilates blood vessels and increases blood flow:
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is synthesised in human body that dilates blood vessels and increases blood flow:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. ** Nitric oxide (NO) is synthesized in the human body and plays a crucial role in vasodilation, which increases blood flow.
The body produces nitric oxide from the amino acid L-arginine using the enzyme nitric oxide synthase (NOS). The primary site of NO synthesis is the inner layer of blood vessels (the endothelium), although other cell types also contribute to its production. Nitric oxide (NO) acts as a powerful vasodilator, relaxing vascular smooth muscles in arteries. This dilation increases blood flow, benefiting overall cardiovascular health. It also serves as a neurotransmitter and is involved in various functions, including neuronal activity and learning.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to the immune system in humans, which of the following statements with regard to B-lymphocytes is/are correct?
I. They produce antibodies in response to pathogens into our blood to fight with them.
II. The immune response mediated by B-lymphocytes is known as humoral immunity.
III. They are primarily responsible for rejection of organ transplants/grafts in the human body.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: With reference to the immune system in humans, which of the following statements with regard to B-lymphocytes is/are correct? I. They produce antibodies in response to pathogens into our blood to fight with them. II. The immune response mediated by B-lymphocytes is known as humoral immunity. III. They are primarily responsible for rejection of organ transplants/grafts in the human body. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. The human body is able to defend itself from most of the foreign agents (disease-causing organisms). This overall ability of the body to fight the disease-causing organisms, conferred by the immune system is called immunity. Immunity is of two types: (i) Innate immunity and (ii) Acquired immunity. Innate immunity is a non-specific type of defence that is present at the time of birth. This is accomplished by providing different types of barriers to the entry of the foreign agents into our body. Innate immunity consists of four types of barriers i.e. physical barriers, physiological barriers, cellular barriers and cytokine barriers.**Acquired immunity, **on the other hand,**is pathogen specific.**It is characterised by memory. This means when**our body **encounters a pathogen for the first time it produces a response called primary response which is of low intensity. **Subsequent encounters with the same pathogen elicits a highly intensified secondary or anamnestic response.**This is ascribed to the fact that our body appears to have memory of the first encounter.**The primary and secondary **immune responses are carried out with the help of two special types of lymphocytes present in our blood, i.e., B
- **lymphocytes and T-lymphocytes. **

Statement I is correct**. The B-lymphocytes produce an army of proteins in response to pathogens into our blood to fight with them. These proteins are called antibodies. These antibodies bind only to specific foreign substances called**antigens**. This binding happens mainly in blood and body fluids. Since this immune protection works through antibodies, it is called**antibody-mediated immunity. **

Statement II is correct**. There are two types of acquired immune response in the human body – one is antibody mediated which is caused by B-lyphocytes and the second type is called cell-mediated immune response or cell-mediated immunity (CMI). The T-lymphocytes mediate CMI. The** immune response mediated by B lymphocytes is also known as humoral immunity **because the**antibodies they **produce circulate in body fluids such as blood and lymph**. Since this protection is provided through soluble factors present in these fluids, it is termed the**humoral immune response**.**

Statement III is incorrect**.**Rejection of transplanted organs is primarily mediated by T-lymphocytes, not B
- **lymphocytes.**During transplant rejection, the recipient’s immune system recognizes donor tissue as foreign and mounts a**cell-mediated immune response**. T cells play the central role in identifying and attacking graft cells.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?lebo1=7-13 – Chapter 7 - Human Health and Disease – Acquired Immunity - Page 135; https://nios.ac.in/media/documents/dmlt/Microbiology/Lesson-59.pdf - Immunology Structure and Function of Immune System – Introduction - Page 516

statementLines: With reference to the hormones in human body, consider the following information:
Hormone Primary Function Secreted by (Gland)
I. Insulin Stimulating the breakdown of glycogen into glucose in the liver Pancreas
II. Glucagon Facilitating the cellular uptake of glucose from the bloodstream Thyroid Gland
III. Cortisol Regulating the body's stress response and metabolism Adrenal Gland
IV. Melatonin Coordinating the circadian rhythm and sleep-wake cycles Pineal Gland In
how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to the hormones in human body, consider the following information: Hormone Primary Function Secreted by (Gland) I. Insulin Stimulating the breakdown of glycogen into glucose in the liver Pancreas II. Glucagon Facilitating the cellular uptake of glucose from the bloodstream Thyroid Gland III. Cortisol Regulating the body's stress response and metabolism Adrenal Gland IV. Melatonin Coordinating the circadian rhythm and sleep-wake cycles Pineal Gland In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Row I is incorrect: Insulin **Insulin is a peptide hormone secreted by the β**- cells of the pancreas.**It plays a major role in the **regulation of glucose**
**homeostasis**. Its main role is to **lower blood glucose levels**. **Its primary functions are: 1. Promoting glycogenesis:**conversion of glucose into glycogen for storage in liver and muscle cells. 2. Facilitating cellular uptake of glucose:**helps cells absorb glucose for energy production. 3. Inhibiting glycogenolysis: prevents breakdown of glycogen into glucose.** 4. Enhances fat synthesis and protein synthesis. It does not stimulate the breakdown of glycogen.**The breakdown of glycogen into glucose (glycogenolysis) is the action of **glucagon, not insulin**.
**Row II is incorrect: Glucagon Glucagon is secreted by the α**
- cells of the pancreas in response to low blood glucose levels. **Its main functions are: **1. Stimulating glycogenolysis: breaking down glycogen into glucose**in the liver to increase blood sugar. 2. Promoting gluconeogenesis: **formation of glucose from amino acids and other non-carbohydrate precursors. 3. Promotes**fat breakdown**(lipolysis) to provide energy. It does**not**facilitate cellular uptake of glucose. Uptake of glucose is mainly promoted by insulin**. Row III is correct: Cortisol **Cortisol is a steroid hormone secreted by the adrenal cortex in Adrenal Gland. It is often called the “stress hormone”. **Its main Functions include: **1. Metabolic regulation: **Increases gluconeogenesis in the liver, mobilizes amino acids from muscles, and promotes lipolysis to increase energy availability. 2. Stress response: **Prepares the body to handle stress by maintaining blood glucose, regulating blood pressure, and modulating immune response. 3. Anti-inflammatory action: **Suppresses inflammation and immune reactions in stress situations. Row IV is correct: Melatonin **Melatonin is a hormone secreted by the pineal gland in response to darkness. **Its main Functions include: **1. Regulating circadian rhythms: **helps synchronize the body’s internal clock with the day-night cycle. 2. Inducing sleep: **signals the brain that it is time to sleep, promoting restorative sleep cycles. 3. Seasonal rhythms: **in some animals, it helps control reproductive cycles according to day length.

statementLines: Consider the following pairs:
Food component (Vitamin/Mineral) Associated diseases/disorder due to its deficiency

questions.questionText: Consider the following pairs: Food component (Vitamin/Mineral) Associated diseases/disorder due to its deficiency I. Iron Goitre II. Vitamin C Scurvy III. Vitamin K Beri beri IV. Calcium Tooth and Bone decay How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Pair I is incorrect: **Iron is an important mineral which is required in small amounts by the body and is therefore also called a trace element. Iron plays a major role in the synthesis of haemoglobin which is the red coloured pigment present in our red blood cells.**Iron deficiency leads to Anaemia (not Goitre).**The**deficiency of iodine leads to a condition **called Goitre**. Pair II is correct:**Vitamin C**, also known as**ascorbic acid**, is sensitive to heat and light and is easily destroyed on exposure to them. It plays a crucial role in the healthy development of teeth, gums, and skin, and enhances the body’s immunity against infections**. Deficiency of Vitamin C causes scurvy**, which is characterized by painful and swollen gums, bleeding, loosening of teeth, and eventual tooth loss. Pair III is incorrect: Vitamin K**is essential for blood clotting and helps prevent excessive bleeding following an injury or cut.**Deficiency of Vitamin K results in delayed clot formation and increased blood loss (but not Beri beri). **Deficiency of Vitamin B₁**(thiamine) leads to a disease known as beri-beri.**

**Pair IV is correct: Calcium** is present in large amounts in the human body and is found in nearly all cells. Its primary function is the formation and maintenance of strong bones and teeth. **Its deficiency causes tooth and bone decay.** Deficiency of calcium can also lead to symptoms like rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults (especially women), resulting in weak and fragile bones.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Consider the following diseases:
I. Sickle-cell anemia
II. Thalassemia
III. Tuberculosis
IV. Phenylketonuria
How many of the above are generally referred to as genetic disorders?
questions.questionText: Consider the following diseases: I. Sickle-cell anemia II. Thalassemia III. Tuberculosis IV. Phenylketonuria How many of the above are generally referred to as genetic disorders?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. Broadly, genetic disorders are classified into Mendelian disorders and chromosomal disorders. Mendelian disorders arise due to mutation or alteration in a single gene and are inherited according to the principles of inheritance, with their transmission pattern traceable through pedigree analysis. Common examples of Mendelian disorders include**haemophilia,**cystic fibrosis,**sickle-cell anaemia**, colour blindness,**phenylketonuria, and thalassemia. ** Option I is correct: Sickle-cell anaemia is an autosomal recessive genetic disorder transmitted to offspring when **both parents are carriers (heterozygous) of the defective gene. **The condition is governed by a single gene with two alleles, HbA (normal) and HbS (mutant). Individuals with the heterozygous genotype (HbA HbS) usually appear normal but act as carriers of the disease and may pass the mutant gene to their children with a 50% probability, a condition known as the sickle-cell trait.** Option II is correct:**Thalassemia is also an autosome-linked recessive blood disease transmitted from parents to the **offspring when both the partners are carriers for the gene (or heterozygous). **The defect could be due to either mutation or deletion which ultimately results in reduced rate of synthesis of one of the globin chains (α and β chains) that make up haemoglobin.  Option III is incorrect: **Tuberculosis (TB) is**not a genetic disorder**because it is**not caused by a mutation in genes or **chromosomes**. It is an**infectious disease**caused by the bacterium**Mycobacterium tuberculosis**. The disease spreads through**airborne droplets**when an infected person coughs or sneezes.** Option IV is correct:**Phenylketonuria is an inborn error of metabolism and is also inherited as the autosomal **recessive trait**. The affected individual lacks an enzyme that converts the amino acid phenylalanine into tyrosine. As a result of this, phenylalanine is accumulated and converted into phenylpyruvic acid and other derivatives. Accumulation of these in the brain results in mental retardation. These are also excreted through urine because of its poor absorption by the kidney.

statementLines: With reference to general biology, Thymine, Guanine and Cytosine are the-
questions.questionText: With reference to general biology, Thymine, Guanine and Cytosine are the-

options.a: Hormones involved in the regulation of metabolism.
options.b: Nitrogenous bases that are components of nucleic acids.
options.c: Medicinal plant varieties used in cancer treatment.
options.d: Amino acids that serve as the building blocks of proteins.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Thymine, Guanine, and Cytosine are**nitrogenous bases**,**which are fundamental components of nucleic acids.** Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) is a long polymer made up of repeating units called deoxyribonucleotides, and its length is defined by the number of nucleotides or base pairs it contains, which is characteristic of each organism. Each nucleotide consists of three parts: a nitrogenous base, a pentose sugar (deoxyribose in DNA and ribose in RNA), and a phosphate group. Nitrogenous bases are of two types—purines (Adenine and Guanine) and pyrimidines (Cytosine, Thymine, and Uracil). Cytosine is common to both DNA and RNA, Thymine is found only in DNA, and Uracil replaces Thymine in RNA. Hence, Thymine, Guanine, and Cytosine correctly refer to the nitrogenous bases of nucleic acids.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following is considered as ‘good cholesterol’ with reference to individuals facing the risk of cardio-vascular diseases and hypertension?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**High Density Lipoprotein (HDL) is often referred to as “good cholesterol**” because it plays a protective role in cardiovascular health. HDL helps remove excess cholesterol from the bloodstream and carries it back to the liver for processing and elimination. This process is known as reverse cholesterol transport, and it helps prevent the buildup of cholesterol in the arteries, reducing the risk of cardiovascular diseases, such as heart attacks and strokes.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA) and Ribonucleic Acid (RNA), consider the following statements:
I. DNA is found in the nucleus of cells whereas RNA is found only outside the nucleus of cells.
II. DNA is chemically less reactive and structurally more stable than RNA.
III. DNA can directly code for the synthesis of proteins, however, RNA is dependent on DNA for synthesis of proteins.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA) and Ribonucleic Acid (RNA), consider the following statements: I. DNA is found in the nucleus of cells whereas RNA is found only outside the nucleus of cells. II. DNA is chemically less reactive and structurally more stable than RNA. III. DNA can directly code for the synthesis of proteins, however, RNA is dependent on DNA for synthesis of proteins. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Both Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA) and Ribonucleic Acid (RNA) act as genetic material in organisms, but they differ in their structure and biological functions.**

Statement I is incorrect: While DNA is found in the nucleus of cells, RNA is found in the nucleus as well as outside **the nucleus **such as cytoplasm, and ribosomes of cells. 

Statement II is correct: **The genetic material should be stable enough not to change with different stages of life cycle, age or with change in physiology of the organism.** Stability is one of the important properties of genetic material. DNA **chemically is less reactive and structurally more stable when compared to RNA. Therefore, among the two nucleic **acids, the DNA is a better genetic material. **

Statement III is incorrect:** RNA can directly code for the synthesis of proteins, hence can easily express the characters. DNA, however, is dependent on RNA for synthesis of proteins. The protein synthesising machinery has evolved around RNA. The above discussion indicates that both RNA and DNA can function as genetic material, but DNA being more stable is preferred for storage of genetic information. For the transmission of genetic information, RNA is better.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to colour blindness in humans, consider the following statements:
Statement I: Compared to females, the occurrence of colour blindness is higher among males.
Statement II: Genes responsible for colour blindness are located on the X chromosome.
Which of the above is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: With reference to colour blindness in humans, consider the following statements: Statement I: Compared to females, the occurrence of colour blindness is higher among males. Statement II: Genes responsible for colour blindness are located on the X chromosome. Which of the above is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I.
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct.
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. Human beings have 23 pairs of chromosomes. One pair decides the sex of the individual. Females have two X chromosomes (XX), while males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY). colour blindness is a sex-linked recessive disorder due to defects in either red or green cones of the eye resulting in failure to discriminate between red and green colour. This defect is due to mutation in certain genes present in the X chromosome.**Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.**Colour blindness is found **much more commonly in males than in females**. It occurs in about** 8 per cent of males and only about 0.4 per cent of **females**. This is because the** genes that lead to red-green colour blindness are on the X chromosome**. Males have only one X chromosome and females have two. The son of a woman who carries the gene has a 50 per cent chance of being colour blind. The mother is not herself colour blind because the gene is recessive. That means that its effect is suppressed by her matching dominant normal gene. A daughter will not normally be colour blind, unless her mother is a carrier and her father is colour blind.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?lebo1=4-13 – Unit VII – Genetics and Evolution – Chapter 4 - Principles of Inheritance and Variation - Genetic Disorders – Colour Blindness - Page 73

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to gene expression in living organisms, which of the following best describes the process of making
(Ribonucleic acid) RNA from (Deoxyribonucleic acid) DNA?
questions.questionText: With reference to gene expression in living organisms, which of the following best describes the process of making (Ribonucleic acid) RNA from (Deoxyribonucleic acid) DNA?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. Living cells function because genetic information stored in DNA is**selectively expressed**. This expression does not happen in one step. It occurs in a**logical sequence**, where DNA first gives rise to RNA, and RNA later helps in protein formation.**

Option a is incorrect. Replication**is the process of copying DNA to form**two identical DNA molecules**. It occurs before cell division and has nothing to do with RNA formation.**

Option b is incorrect. Translation**is the next step after transcription. It refers to the process by which the**RNA **sequence is decoded to form a protein**.**

Option c is correct**.**The process of copying genetic information from one strand of the DNA into RNA is termed as **transcription.**DNA stores all instructions of the body, but it cannot directly take part in protein formation. For this reason, the cell first prepares an RNA copy of the required information. This RNA acts as a messenger that carries the instruction forward. During transcription,**only one strand of DNA is used**to make RNA. The cell follows this rule to avoid confusion in genetic information. If both strands of DNA were copied at the same time, they would produce RNA molecules with different information. This would result in the formation of different proteins from the same DNA region, which would disturb normal cell functioning.**

Option d is incorrect. Reverse transcription**is a special process seen in some viruses, where**RNA is used to make **DNA**. It is not the normal pathway of gene expression in most living organisms.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?lebo1=5-13 – Chapter 5 - Molecular Basis of Inheritance – Transcription – Page 91

It produces about 1.5 litres of alkaline pancreatic juice per day, full of enzymes necessary for us to break down food.**Pancreatic juice consists of protein-digesting trypsin **and chymotrypsin,**fat-digesting lipases **and**carbohydrate digesting amylases. **Pancreatic enzymes are released into the duodenum. questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Which of the following best describes the nature of satellite deoxyribonucleic acid (Satellite DNA)?
questions.questionText: Which of the following best describes the nature of satellite deoxyribonucleic acid (Satellite DNA)?

options.a: Unique DNA segments that differ completely between all individuals.
options.b: DNA sequences that actively code for structural and enzymatic proteins.
options.c: Highly repetitive DNA sequences that do not code for proteins.
options.d: DNA regions found only in germ cells and absent in somatic cells.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. The human genome is largely similar across individuals, yet every person is genetically unique. This uniqueness arises mainly from variations present in certain repetitive regions of DNA. These regions do not perform direct protein-coding functions, but they play a critical role in identification techniques such as**DNA fingerprinting**.**Satellite DNA**consists of**short DNA sequences repeated many times**in the genome. These sequences**do not code for **proteins**, but they occupy a significant portion of human DNA. Their number of repeats varies greatly from one individual to another. This high degree of variation, called**polymorphism**, makes satellite DNA the foundation of DNA fingerprinting.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?lebo1=5-13 – Chapter 5 - Molecular Basis of Inheritance – DNA Fingerprinting - Page 105

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to the applications of DNA fingerprinting, consider the following:
I. Establishing biological relationships such as parentage
II. Establishing identity of individuals in a criminal investigation
III. Comparing genetic material of extinct species with living organisms For
how many of the above can DNA fingerprinting be effectively used presently?
questions.questionText: With reference to the applications of DNA fingerprinting, consider the following: I. Establishing biological relationships such as parentage II. Establishing identity of individuals in a criminal investigation III. Comparing genetic material of extinct species with living organisms For how many of the above can DNA fingerprinting be effectively used presently?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. DNA fingerprinting is a technique that identifies individuals based on unique patterns in their DNA. It works on the principle that certain regions of DNA, especially**VNTRs (Variable Number Tandem Repeats)**, show high variation among individuals. These variations are stable, inheritable, and identical in all cells of an individual. It is widely used in medicine, forensics, and biological research.** Option I is correct**. VNTR patterns are inherited from parents to offspring. By analysing and matching these patterns, parental relationships such as**paternity and maternity**can be accurately established. Even parental DNA profiles can be reconstructed using offspring data, making this method reliable in disputed cases.** Option II is correct**. DNA fingerprinting plays a crucial role in**forensic science**. DNA collected from blood, hair, skin, semen, or other biological materials at a crime scene can be analysed and compared with that of suspects. This helps in establishing**guilt or innocence**with a high degree of certainty in criminal cases.** Option III is correct**. DNA fingerprinting is used in**evolutionary biology**. By comparing DNA extracted from fossils with DNA of modern organisms, scientists can study evolutionary relationships and genetic changes over time.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?lebo1=5-13 – Chapter 5 - Molecular Basis of Inheritance – DNA Fingerprinting - Page 107; https://ncert.nic.in/textbook/pdf/lebt104.pdf - Chapter 4 - Applications of Recombinant DNA Technology - Applications of DNA Fingerprinting – Page 70

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to theories of evolution in biology, which of the following best describes the term ‘saltation’?
questions.questionText: With reference to theories of evolution in biology, which of the following best describes the term ‘saltation’?

options.a: Sudden formation of new species due to large mutations occurring in a species in a single step.
options.b: Gradual accumulation of small heritable variations in a species over many generations.
options.c: Evolution driven only by natural selection acting on minor variations in species.
options.d: Random genetic drift occurring in small and isolated ecological populations.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. Evolutionary biology has attempted to explain how variation arises and how new species are formed. While Darwin emphasised slow and continuous change, later scientists proposed alternative explanations. One such idea came from Hugo de Vries, whose views differed sharply from Darwinian gradualism.**Saltation**(single step large mutation) refers to the idea that**new species arise suddenly due to large mutations **occurring in a single step**. This concept was proposed by**Hugo de Vries**, who believed that mutations, rather than small continuous variations, were the main driving force of evolution. According to him, such large and sudden changes could directly lead to speciation.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to immunity in humans, consider the following:
I. Production of antibodies by B-lymphocytes
II. Saliva present in the mouth
III. Cytokine barriers such as interferons
IV. Skin acting as a physical barrier
V. Tears secreted by the eyes
How many of the above are examples of innate immunity?
questions.questionText: With reference to immunity in humans, consider the following: I. Production of antibodies by B-lymphocytes II. Saliva present in the mouth III. Cytokine barriers such as interferons IV. Skin acting as a physical barrier V. Tears secreted by the eyes How many of the above are examples of innate immunity?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. The human body is able to defend itself from most of the foreign agents (disease-causing organisms). This overall ability of the body to fight the disease-causing organisms, conferred by the immune system is called immunity. Immunity is of two types: (i) Innate immunity and (ii) Acquired immunity. Innate immunity is a non-specific type of defence that is present at the time of birth. This is accomplished by providing different types of barriers to the entry of the foreign agents into our body.**Innate immunity consists of four types of **barriers i.e. physical barriers, physiological barriers, cellular barriers and cytokine barriers. ** Option I is incorrect**.**Antibodies**by B-lymphocytes are produced only**after exposure to a specific antigen**. Their formation involves immune memory and specificity, which are defining features of**Acquired**immunity**.** Option II is correct**.**Saliva**is another**physiological barrier**. It helps in flushing out microbes from the oral cavity and contains enzymes that restrict the growth of microorganisms. This protective role does not depend on prior infection and therefore belongs to innate immunity.** Option III is correct**.**Interferons**are**cytokines released by virus-infected cells**. They protect nearby healthy cells by preventing viral replication. This response is rapid, non-specific, and does not involve immune memory. Hence, cytokine barriers are an essential component of innate immunity.** Option IV is correct**.**Skin**is the**first and most important physical barrier**of innate immunity. It prevents the entry of pathogens into the body by forming a continuous protective covering. As this protection is always present and non-specific, skin is a classic example of innate immunity.** Option V is correct**.**Tears**form a**physiological barrier**of innate immunity. They continuously wash the surface of the eyes and contain substances that inhibit microbial growth. This action is non-specific and operates from birth, which makes tears an important component of innate immunity. Knowledge Base:**Acquired immunity **is**pathogen specific**. It is characterised by**memory**. This means when our body encounters a pathogen for the first time it produces a response called primary response which is of low intensity. Subsequent encounters with the same pathogen elicits a highly intensified secondary or anamnestic response. The primary and secondary immune responses are carried out with the help of two special types of lymphocytes present in our blood, i.e.,**B-lymphocytes**and**T-lymphocytes**. The B-lymphocytes produce an army of proteins in response to pathogens into our blood to fight with them. These proteins are called antibodies.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?lebo1=7-13 – Chapter 7 - Human Health and Disease – Innate Immunity - Page 134

which one of the following parts of the plant is used?

Saffron crocuses are native to the**Mediterranean region**, but they are now cultivated in many parts of the world, including**Iran, India, **Spain, and Morocco**.** Iran is the world’s largest producer of saffron.**In India,**Saffron**is mainly found in the**Pampore **region of Jammu and Kashmir, India**. It is also cultivated in some other parts of India, such as**Himachal Pradesh and **Uttarakhand.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: Which among the following organisms primarily carries out respiration through its moist skin?
questions.questionText: Which among the following organisms primarily carries out respiration through its moist skin?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

Option a is correct:**Earthworms have no respiratory organs**. The entire skin functions as the respiratory surface.**Earthworms respire mainly through their moist, permeable skin**, a process known as**cutaneous respiration**. Their skin is richly supplied with tiny blood capillaries. -**Oxygen from the surrounding air dissolves in the thin layer of moisture present on the skin**and then diffuses directly into the blood capillaries, while**carbon dioxide diffuses out in the same manner**. Hence, maintaining a moist body surface is essential for their survival; earthworms die if their skin dries out.**

Option b is incorrect:**Lizards (reptiles) breathe using lungs through pulmonary respiration**, with air entering via nostrils and moving to lungs. They**do not rely on skin for primary gas exchange**(though minor cutaneous respiration occurs in some reptiles, it is not primary). 

Option c is incorrect:**Crabs respire through gills**, which are located in branchial chambers on either side of the body. These gills extract dissolved oxygen from water. Even in semi-terrestrial crabs, respiration mainly takes place through moist gills, not through the general body skin. Hence,**crabs do not depend on skin for gaseous exchange. **

Option d is incorrect**:**Cockroaches respire through a well-developed tracheal system**, where air enters the body via openings called spiracles and is directly transported to tissues through tracheae and tracheoles, without the involvement of blood. The**skin does not participate in respiration. **

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to the human immune system, consider the following:
I. An infant receiving antibodies through the mother’s first milk.
II. An injection of ready-made antibodies administered after a snake bite.
III. Injecting a weakened virus to stimulate the body’s immune system.
IV. Antibodies transferred from the mother to the foetus through the placenta during pregnancy.
How many of the above are examples of passive immunity?
questions.questionText: With reference to the human immune system, consider the following: I. An infant receiving antibodies through the mother’s first milk. II. An injection of ready-made antibodies administered after a snake bite. III. Injecting a weakened virus to stimulate the body’s immune system. IV. Antibodies transferred from the mother to the foetus through the placenta during pregnancy. How many of the above are examples of passive immunity?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. When a host is exposed to antigens, which may be in the form of living or dead microbes or other proteins, antibodies are produced in the host body. This type of immunity is called**active immunity**. When**ready-made antibodies are **directly given to protect the body against foreign agents, it is called passive immunity**.**

Statement I is correct**. The** yellowish fluid colostrum secreted by mother**during the initial days of lactation has**abundant antibodies (IgA)**to protect the infant. This is an example of**passive immunity**.**

Statement II is correct**. In case of a snake (like cobra) bite,**ready-made antibodies (antivenom)**are injected into the body. These antibodies act immediately to neutralise the venom. Since the body does not generate these antibodies itself, this is**passive immunity**.**

Statement III is incorrect**.**Vaccines**containing weakened or inactivated pathogens stimulate the body to**produce its **own antibodies**. This process takes time and results in immune memory. Hence, this is an example of**active immunity**.**

Statement IV is correct**. During pregnancy,**antibodies from the mother pass to the foetus through the placenta**. This provides protection to the newborn during the initial months of life. Since the antibodies are transferred and not produced by the fetus, this is** passive immunity**.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?lebo1=7-13 – Chapter 7 - Human Health and Disease – Active and Passive Immunity - Page 136

questions.subject: Science & Technology
statementLines: With reference to biology,
which one of the following best describes the idea of ‘genetic equilibrium’?
questions.questionText: With reference to biology, which one of the following best describes the idea of ‘genetic equilibrium’?

options.a: A state where the gene pool of a population remains constant over generations in the absence of evolutionary forces.
options.b: The condition in which all individuals of a population possess identical genetic makeup.
options.c: The stage at which a species reaches its maximum sustainable population size in an ecosystem.
options.d: A condition in which an ecological community has an equal number of individuals of all species.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. Genetic equilibrium**refers to a condition in which the**allele frequencies**of a population**remain constant from one **generation to the next**. This idea comes from the**Hardy–Weinberg principle**. It explains how genes behave in an ideal population. In genetic equilibrium,** the gene pool, which means the total collection of genes and their alleles in a population**, remains stable over time. Individual organisms may differ genetically, but the overall proportion of alleles does not change. This stability is maintained**only when no evolutionary forces act**on the population. These forces include**natural **selection**,**mutation**,**gene flow**,**genetic drift**, and**recombination**. If none of these forces disturb the population, the genetic structure remains unchanged. Genetic equilibrium does**not mean absence of variation**. Variation exists in the population, but it is**balanced**. The population is therefore**not evolving**under this condition. If the**allele frequencies change**, it means the**genetic **equilibrium is disturbed**. Such a disturbance indicates that**evolution is taking place**. Knowledge Base: An**allele** is a different version of a gene found at the same position on chromosomes. For example, the gene for eye color has alleles for brown, blue, or green eyes.

statementLines: When the bark of a tree is removed in a circular fashion all around near its base, it gradually dries up or dies because-

options.c: Tree is infected by the soil microbes

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. ** When the bark of a tree is removed in a circular fashion all around near its base, the phloem tissue is damaged. Phloem tissue is responsible for transporting food from the leaves to the roots. Without phloem tissue, the roots cannot receive the food they need to survive.

statementLines: With reference to Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS), consider the following statements:
Statement I: It is an autoimmune disorder.
Statement II: In GBS, the immune system may destroy the fatty layer wrapped around nerve cells.

questions.questionText: With reference to Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS), consider the following statements: Statement I: It is an autoimmune disorder. Statement II: In GBS, the immune system may destroy the fatty layer wrapped around nerve cells. Statement III: Presently, there is no known cure for GBS. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and Statement III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct: Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS) is a rare but serious autoimmune disorder that **affects the peripheral nervous system, leading to muscle weakness, loss of reflexes, and even paralysis.**

Statement II is correct: In Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS), the immune system destroys the myelin sheath
- - a fatty **layer wrapped around nerve cells. **This impacts the nerves’ ability to send signals to the brain, which causes weakness in the muscles. GBS affects the peripheral nervous system - - the part of the nervous system outside the brain and spinal cord - - that controls the movement of muscles, temperature, touch and pain sensations.**Statement II explains Statement I: In autoimmune conditions, the body’s immune system begins to attack its own **cells. Similarly, in GBS, the immune system destroys the myelin sheath, which impacts the nerve's ability to send **signals to the brain. Thus, it is also considered an autoimmune disorder. **

Statement III is correct: Guillain-Barré Syndrome is a rare but potentially life-threatening neurological disorder. **There is no known cure for GBS, **only the timely medical intervention can alleviate symptoms, reduce complications, and accelerate the recovery process.**Statement III does not explain Statement I:** Though for most of the autoimmune disorder, there is no known cure and only supportive care can be provided. But no known cure does not explain why GBS is considered an autoimmune disease. It is an autoimmune disease as the body’s own immune system begins to attack its own cells.

statementLines: With reference to cell organelles and their primary functions, consider the following pairs:
Cell Organelle Primary Function
I. Endoplasmic Reticulum Synthesis of proteins and lipids
II. Lysosomes Intracellular digestion and waste removal
III. Ribosomes Modification and packaging of proteins
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to cell organelles and their primary functions, consider the following pairs: Cell Organelle Primary Function I. Endoplasmic Reticulum Synthesis of proteins and lipids II. Lysosomes Intracellular digestion and waste removal III. Ribosomes Modification and packaging of proteins How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Pair I is correct:**The Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER) is an extensive membranous network that plays a central role in biosynthetic activities of the cell.**It exists in two forms: **-**Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (RER) has ribosomes attached to its surface**and is primarily**involved in the **synthesis of proteins**, especially those destined for secretion, membranes, or lysosomes. -**Smooth Endoplasmic Reticulum (SER) lacks ribosomes**and is mainly**responsible for the synthesis of lipids,**phospholipids, and steroids, along with**detoxification**of drugs and poisons.**Pair II is correct:**Lysosomes are**membrane-bound organelles**formed by the process of packaging in the golgi apparatus. -**Lysosomes contain hydrolytic enzymes**(such as proteases, lipases and carbohydrases) that**break down**waste materials, cellular debris, and foreign particles and are**capable of digesting**carbohydrates, proteins, lipids and nucleic acids. - Lysosomes are therefore known as the “**suicide bags”**of the cell and are**central to intracellular digestion and **cellular waste removal. **Pair III is incorrect: **Ribosomes are composed of ribonucleic acid (RNA) and proteins and are not surrounded by any membrane. - Their**primary and exclusive function is the synthesis of proteins**by translating messenger RNA (mRNA) into polypeptide chains, and**not Modification and packaging of proteins. **-**Modification and packaging of proteins are functions of the Golgi apparatus**, which processes proteins synthesized by ribosomes before they are transported to their final destinations.

Heart Disease Description
I. Heart failure It is a condition when the heart muscle is suddenly damaged by an inadequate blood supply
II. Cardiac arrest It is a condition when heart is not pumping blood effectively enough to meet the needs of the body
III. Heart attack It is a condition when the heart stops beating

questions.questionText: With reference to human health, consider the following pairs: Heart Disease Description I. Heart failure It is a condition when the heart muscle is suddenly damaged by an inadequate blood supply II. Cardiac arrest It is a condition when heart is not pumping blood effectively enough to meet the needs of the body III. Heart attack It is a condition when the heart stops beating How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Pairs I, II and III are incorrect: **Heart failure means the state of heart when it is not pumping blood effectively enough to meet the needs of the body. It is sometimes called congestive heart failure because congestion of the lungs is one of the main symptoms of this disease.
Heart failure is not the same as cardiac arrest (when the heart stops beating) or a heart attack (when the heart muscle is suddenly damaged by an inadequate blood supply).
